metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC2, name the two commit-units explicitly (e.g. 'only gitattributes and gitignore commit; env-id, gc-config, and merge-ours do not') so the per-scenario commit-count assertion is unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC2 references 'the two commit-units' without naming which two of the five ensure units (env-id, gc-config, merge-ours, gitattributes, gitignore) involve git commits. The drift setup describes three committed files (gitattributes, gitignore, .env-id), so an executor cannot write the required 'only the two commit-units commit, and only when behind' assertion without resolving this externally.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC2: 'only the two commit-units commit, and only when behind'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'only the two commit-units commit' with 'only the gitattributes and gitignore units commit (env-id, gc-config, merge-ours are non-committing); assert git log shows exactly N new commits where N = count of commit-units that were behind in that scenario.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC2, replace 'the two commit-units' with an explicit enumeration: name `gitattributes` and `gitignore` as the units that issue `git commit`, and name `env-id`, `gc-config`, and `merge-ours` as config/file-only units that never commit — so the git-log assertion bounds are unambiguous for implementers.
findings.finding: AC2 references 'the two commit-units' without naming which two of the five ensure units issue git commits. From the actual source, only `gitattributes` (_gitattributes_unit, init.py:476-505) and `gitignore` (_gitignore_unit, init.py:399-429) run `git commit`; the other three (`env-id`, `gc-config`, `merge-ours`) mutate only local files or git config and never commit. A test implementer relying solely on the plan text cannot determine which units to target in git-log assertions for 'commit-only-when-behind', and could under- or over-assert commits.
findings.location: AC2 — 'only the two commit-units commit, and only when behind'
findings.suggested_fix: AC2 revised: 'only the two commit-units (`gitattributes` and `gitignore`) add git commits, and only when their respective drift is present; `env-id`, `gc-config`, and `merge-ours` never commit (config/file-only units).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the two commit-units explicitly in AC-2 (they are `gitattributes` and `gitignore`; the other three — `env-id`, `gc-config`, `merge-ours` — make no git commits) so git-log assertions are unambiguously specified.
findings.finding: AC-2 asserts 'only the two commit-units commit, and only when behind' but never names which two of the five ensure units are commit-units. From code inspection, `gitattributes` and `gitignore` call `git commit` internally; `env-id` writes a filesystem file (no commit), `gc-config` and `merge-ours` call `git config` (no commit). Without this being explicit in the plan, an implementer may assert on the wrong pair or on git-log commit-counts incorrectly, and future unit additions won't know which category they fall into.
findings.location: AC-2: 'only the two commit-units commit, and only when behind'
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC-2 to read: '…only the two commit-units (`gitattributes` and `gitignore`) commit, and only when behind; the three non-commit units (`env-id`, `gc-config`, `merge-ours`) never increment the git log regardless of drift state.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: Name the two commit-units explicitly (gitignore and gitattributes) so the git-log assertion in each parametrized scenario can be computed without source inspection.
findings.finding: AC2 asserts 'only the two commit-units commit, and only when behind' but the plan never defines which two of the five ensure units are 'commit-units'. From source, gitignore and gitattributes call `git commit`; env-id, gc-config, and merge-ours do not. This decision rule is referenced in the AC but not co-located with the unit descriptions in the What section — an implementer writing the git-log assertion cannot determine the expected commit delta without consulting the source.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'only the two commit-units commit, and only when behind'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section or AC2: 'commit-units are gitignore and gitattributes (the two units that write and commit blobs to the tickets branch); env-id, gc-config, and merge-ours are non-commit-units (they mutate local git-config or working-tree files only)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define explicitly in the What section (or a glossary) which of the five ensure units are 'commit-units' (i.e., apply changes via git commit), and confirm whether env-id restoration commits — then update AC-2 to name them.
findings.finding: AC-2 asserts 'only the two commit-units commit' but the plan body never explicitly identifies which two of the five ensure units are commit-units. The What section describes 'committed .gitattributes' and 'stale committed .gitignore' (implying these two), but also 'deleted .env-id' — restoring a git-tracked deleted file typically also requires a commit, suggesting possibly three commit-units. An implementer cannot resolve this from the plan text alone.
findings.location: AC-2 ('only the two commit-units commit') vs. What section (drift fabrication list)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What section: 'Commit-units are gitattributes and gitignore (changes applied via git commit); all other units mutate config/files without committing. Env-id is written as an untracked file and does not commit.' Adjust AC-2 accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC4, make 'no new commits' explicit: specify that the test must assert `git rev-list --count tickets` is unchanged after the second `--repair`, mirroring the git-log assertion already required by AC2/AC3 for the parametrized scenarios.
findings.finding: AC4 asserts the second `rebar fsck --repair` on a fully-legacy store 'reports `0 changed` / no new commits', but the plan does not specify how 'no new commits' is verified in the operator path. The parametrized scenarios (AC2/AC3) explicitly say 'git-log assertion', but AC4 only implies it via the slash notation. The fsck output itself does not include a commit count; it only emits 'N changed, N failed'. Without an explicit git-log assertion, the 'no new commits' invariant in AC4 is unverifiable from the output string alone.
findings.location: AC4 — 'a second `--repair` reports `0 changed` / no new commits'
findings.suggested_fix: AC4 revised: 'a second `--repair` reports `0 changed` in the `ensures: swept …` line AND the `tickets` branch HEAD is unchanged (git-log assertion), confirming no new commits were produced.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Scope S0–S5 to assert only the *cross-unit differential invariant* (exactly the drifted set reports `changed`, others `ok`) without re-fabricating the per-unit drift states from scratch — or explicitly call out in comments that S1/S3/S4 are intentionally re-exercised for combinatorial completeness (not as primary coverage) to justify the duplication.
findings.finding: Single-unit scenarios S1 (legacy-gc), S3 (retired-gitattributes), and S4 (stale-gitignore) rebuild drift-fabrication and assertion logic that is already expressed in landing production tests, violating NIH/Rule-of-Three for those individual cells.
findings.location: What section / Acceptance Criteria AC2 — scenarios S0–S5 in the parametrized matrix
findings.suggested_fix: For S1, S3, S4 matrix cells: delegate the drift-fabrication step to the same helper already proven in the per-unit tests (e.g. import the git-config manipulation as a shared `_fabricate_gc_drift(tracker)` extracted from test_gc_config.py), so the matrix adds the parametric assertion layer without re-implementing the drift logic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm AC4's 'fully-legacy store' scenario adds distinct value over `test_fsck_ensures.py::test_repair_converges_pending_store` (e.g. by asserting each unit's individual `changed` outcome by id, not just the aggregate count), and document that distinction in the test file's module docstring.
findings.finding: AC4 (operator `fsck --repair` on a fully-legacy store, reports 5 changed, second run 0 changed) substantially re-implements what `test_fsck_ensures.py::test_repair_converges_pending_store` + `test_repair_ensure_phase_is_idempotent` already assert, introducing NIH overlap on the operator path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove AC5 from `test_ensure_drift_matrix.py` (the pre-feature/no-marker scenario is already owned by `test_fsck_ensures.py`) or reframe it to test something the existing file does not (e.g. no-marker combined with actual per-unit drift rather than just a missing marker file).
findings.finding: AC5 (pre-feature store / no `.ensure-applied` reported `0/5 applied` by read-only fsck, then reaches `5/5` after --repair) is fully covered by the two existing tests `test_plain_fsck_pending_line_and_no_sweep` and `test_repair_converges_pending_store` in `test_fsck_ensures.py`.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove AC5 as a standalone scenario from the new matrix file and add a comment in the module docstring cross-referencing test_fsck_ensures.py as the owner of the pre-feature-store read-only path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` to the per-test fixture (or as an `autouse` fixture) in `test_ensure_drift_matrix.py`, matching the pattern in all sibling ensure tests, to prevent the module-level `_pending_cache` from leaking across parametrized scenarios.
findings.finding: The plan never mentions calling `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` in the test fixture or between parametrized scenarios, yet every existing sibling test (test_fsck_ensures.py, test_ensure_invariants.py, test_pending_hint.py) calls it to prevent the in-process pending-set cache from leaking state between tests that delete/restore the .ensure-applied marker. Without it, a parametrized test that removes the marker in one scenario will cause the next scenario to see a stale cached empty pending set, making idempotency and pre-feature assertions non-deterministic.
findings.location: What section (fixture setup) and AC2/AC3 (idempotency assertions)
findings.suggested_fix: In the fixture that fabricates drift (called before each parametrized scenario), add `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` immediately after any manipulation of the `.ensure-applied` marker, as done in `test_fsck_ensures.py` line 85 and `test_ensure_invariants.py` line 36.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` to the test fixture (or each scenario's setup) so the per-process pending cache is cleared between parametrized cases, matching the established pattern in `test_fsck_ensures.py`, `test_ensure_invariants.py`, and `test_pending_hint.py`.
findings.finding: The plan's fixture spec omits `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` before each scenario. The `_pending_cache` dict is a process-level singleton that caches `.ensure-applied` reads per tracker path. In a parametrized test run — especially AC-3's idempotency second-sweep assertion and AC-5's pre-feature-store hint check — a stale cache entry from a previous scenario (or from the drift-setup phase itself) can cause the hint subsystem to skip a re-read and report the wrong pending set, making assertions pass for wrong reasons. Every analogous test in the codebase explicitly calls `ensures._reset_pending_cache()` in fixture setup.
findings.location: AC-3 (idempotency), AC-5 (pre-feature store), fixture setup spec
findings.suggested_fix: In the `tmp_path` fixture or at the top of each parametrized test body, call `from rebar._store import ensures; ensures._reset_pending_cache()` before any `run_ensures` or `fsck` invocation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'drift-gate regressions' in AC6 — either name the test suite/command that enforces the drift gate (e.g. `pytest tests/interfaces/store/ -k drift`) or remove the phrase if it is already covered by `make lint` / `make typecheck`.
findings.finding: AC6 refers to 'no drift-gate regressions' without defining what a 'drift-gate' is or how to detect a regression in it; an executor cannot write or run a check for this criterion as stated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria – AC6: 'make lint + make typecheck clean; the new file adds no over-cap module and no drift-gate regressions'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'over-cap module' and 'drift-gate regressions' inline in AC-6 or via a cross-reference, so the criterion is fully verifiable.
findings.finding: AC-6 uses the terms 'over-cap module' and 'drift-gate regressions' without definition anywhere in the plan. These are project-specific concepts whose meaning an implementer (or reviewer) cannot infer from the plan text, making AC-6 only partially verifiable.
findings.location: AC-6: 'the new file adds no over-cap module and no drift-gate regressions'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in AC-4 (or the What section) whether `rebar fsck --repair` is invoked via subprocess or direct function call, and confirm this is compatible with the default-tier hermetic constraint stated in AC-1.
findings.finding: AC-1 requires the test suite to 'run in the default (non-integration/non-external) tier', but AC-4 requires invoking `rebar fsck --repair` as an operator CLI command on a store. CLI subprocess invocation is conventionally an integration-tier concern; placing it in the default tier contradicts the hermetic/unit constraint unless the plan clarifies that `fsck --repair` is invoked as a direct Python function call (not a subprocess).
findings.location: AC-1 (default tier constraint) vs. AC-4 (operator path: `rebar fsck --repair`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to AC-4: 'invoked as a direct Python function call (e.g., `cli_fsck(repair=True, store=tracker)`) to remain within the default hermetic tier — not via subprocess.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief CLAUDE.md (or inline module docstring in `test_ensure_drift_matrix.py`) that documents the drift-matrix convention — what it covers, how to add a new scenario when an ensure-unit is added or changed, and the five-unit registry expectation.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel 'drift-matrix' test pattern and parametrized legacy-fabrication convention for the ensure-registry, but nowhere specifies that this pattern should be documented (e.g., in CLAUDE.md or a test-architecture note), leaving future contributors who add new ensure-units without guidance on how to extend the matrix.
findings.location: What section / AC line 6 ('make lint + make typecheck clean; the new file adds no over-cap module and no drift-gate regressions')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a module-level docstring to `test_ensure_drift_matrix.py` that explains: (1) the drift-matrix pattern and its purpose, (2) the procedure for adding a new scenario when a new ensure-unit lands, (3) the invariant that `ENSURE_UNIT_IDS` in the parametrize decorator must stay in sync with the live registry. Optionally cross-reference from CLAUDE.md under a 'Testing conventions' section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten AC4 to reference the actual fsck output format: assert `'5 changed, 0 failed'` (or `'swept 5 unit(s); 5 changed'`) appears in output, matching the template in fsck.py lines 759–761 and the pattern used by the sibling `test_fsck_ensures.py` (`assert '0 changed' in out`).
findings.finding: AC4 asserts that `rebar fsck --repair` on a fully-legacy store 'reports 5 changed', but the actual fsck output format (fsck.py lines 759–761) is 'ensures: swept N unit(s); M changed, 0 failed' — not a bare '5 changed' string. A test asserting the literal phrase '5 changed' would pass only if the test matches the composite token inside the longer line ('5 changed' IS a substring of '5 changed, 0 failed'), but the plan leaves this ambiguous and could lead an implementor to write an incorrect assertion string.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reports 5 changed' in AC4 with 'output contains `"5 changed, 0 failed"`' (or equivalently `"swept 5 unit(s)"`), mirroring the assertion convention established in test_fsck_ensures.py line 103.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Specify the exact output substring(s) to assert for the fully-legacy fsck --repair path, matching the actual fsck output schema (`"ensures: swept 5 unit(s); 5 changed, 0 failed"` for first run; `"0 changed"` within `"ensures: swept 5 unit(s); 0 changed, 0 failed"` for second run).
findings.finding: AC4 says `rebar fsck --repair` on a fully-legacy store 'reports 5 changed' and a second '--repair reports 0 changed / no new commits', but the actual fsck output schema (fsck.py lines 759–761) emits 'ensures: swept N unit(s); M changed, 0 failed' — no bare '5 changed' or '0 changed' substring exists as a standalone token. An implementer writing the assertion must infer the correct output format from source, and a naive `assert '5 changed' in out` would fail.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC4: 'Operator path: rebar fsck --repair converges a fully-legacy store (reports 5 changed) and a second --repair reports 0 changed / no new commits'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reports 5 changed' with 'output contains `"ensures: swept 5 unit(s); 5 changed, 0 failed"`' and '0 changed / no new commits' with 'output contains `"0 changed"`  (within the swept-units summary line) and git-log unchanged'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
